query: effectiveness of selinexor in multiple myeloma

retrieved_docs.title: Relapsed and Refractory Multiple Myeloma -- Treatment / Management -- First Relapse
retrieved_docs.content: The CANDOR trial showed a 37% reduction in the risk of progression or death in a population with DKd (daratumumab, carfilzomib, and dexamethasone) compared to Kd alone. [47] Efficacy is noted to be similar between twice weekly and weekly dosages according to CANDOR and EQUULEUS trial. APOLLO trial showed 12 month PFS benefit of 52% vs 35% between Daratumumab, pomalidomide, and dexamethasone (DPd) vs pomalidomide and dexamethasone (Pd), respectively. [48]

retrieved_docs.title: Relapsed and Refractory Multiple Myeloma -- Treatment / Management -- Second Relapse or Subsequent Relapse
retrieved_docs.content: In cases of triple-class refractory disease, where patients have failed multiple lines of treatment, including PIs, IMiDs, and mAbs against CD38, recently approved therapies are selinexor and dexamethasone; VdT-PACE (bortezomib, dexamethasone, thalidomide, cisplatin, doxorubicin, cyclophosphamide, and etoposide); melflufen-dexamethasone and CAR-T-cells, idecabtagene, and belantamab mafoditin. The HORIZON study has shown that melflufen plus dexamethasone demonstrates a modicum of activity in relapsed and refractory myeloma, especially in triple-class refractory disease. [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Relapsed and Refractory Multiple Myeloma -- Treatment / Management -- First Relapse
retrieved_docs.content: For patients with MM refractory to both lenalidomide and daratumumab, treatment options include carfilzomib, pomalidomide, and dexamethasone (KPd); bortezomib, pomalidomide, and dexamethasone (VPd); bortezomib, cyclophosphamide, and dexamethasone (VCd or CyBorD) as studied in the EVOLUTION Trial; or selinexor, bortezomib, and dexamethasone (SVd) as studied in the BOSTON Trial. [44] [45] Patients on selinexor should get antiemetic prophylaxis and monitor for thrombocytopenia, neutropenia, and hyponatremia.

retrieved_docs.title: Relapsed and Refractory Multiple Myeloma -- Treatment / Management -- Treatment Options
retrieved_docs.content: Various anti-myeloma drugs and their combinations are used to select treatment plans for RRMM patients. These include autologous stem cell transplantation, protease inhibitors (PIs), immunomodulatory drugs (IMiDs), targeted agents like monoclonal antibodies (mAbs), antibody-drug conjugates, chimeric antigen receptor T-cells (CAR-T-cells), biphasic T-cell engagers (BiTEs), selinexor, venetoclax, and many others.

retrieved_docs.title: Relapsed and Refractory Multiple Myeloma -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Rpn13 has high expression in MM cells and is involved in the regulation, proliferation, and survival of MM cells; therefore, inhibitors that target Rpn13 may play a significant role in treating MM and increasing survival. [57]

retrieved_docs.title: Relapsed and Refractory Multiple Myeloma -- Toxicity and Adverse Effect Management
retrieved_docs.content: Although newer anti-MM agents have significant benefits in treating RRMM, they are not free of adverse effects. Every drug has its unique adverse effect profile and needs to be considered while treating the patient for prompt identification and management. Sometimes the adverse effects are so severe that treatment must be changed or temporarily discontinued. [59] Some of the toxicities of medications being used are as follows: Neurotoxicity: CAR-T cell therapy Ocular toxicity: belantamab mafodotin Cardiotoxicity: carfilzomib, doxorubicin Gastrointestinal toxicity: selinexor, lenalidomide Nephrotoxicity: lenalidomide, zoledronic acid Immune impression: Auto-HCT, CAR-T cell therapy, dexamethasone Myelosuppression: daratumumab, carfilzomib, selinexor, CAR-T cell therapy Peripheral neuropathy: thalidomide, protease inhibitors During the course of treatment, patients are closely monitored for adverse effects and treated accordingly.

retrieved_docs.title: Ixazomib -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Ixazomib, a second-generation proteasome inhibitor, emerges as a pivotal therapeutic option for managing multiple myeloma. Addressing the needs of multiple myeloma patients, this activity discusses ixazomib's mechanism of action, indications, and contraindications. The comprehensive discussion extends to ixazomib's adverse event profile and other critical facets, ensuring the members of an interprofessional team can navigate the complexities inherent in managing multiple myeloma and its associated conditions. The collaborative emphasis throughout underscores the indispensable nature of interprofessional teamwork in managing these intricate hematological conditions and the need to enhance the team's knowledge and proficiency in leveraging ixazomib to benefit individuals affected by multiple myeloma and its related complexities.

retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Multiple Myeloma -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the pathophysiology of multiple myeloma. Review the appropriate steps in evaluating a patient suspected of having multiple myeloma. Summarize the first-line therapy options for multiple myeloma. Outline the adverse effects of the disease process as well as treatment-related side effects. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Ixazomib -- Indications -- FDA Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Ixazomib is currently FDA-approved to treat relapsed or refractory multiple myeloma.

retrieved_docs.title: Relapsed and Refractory Multiple Myeloma -- Treatment / Management -- Second Relapse or Subsequent Relapse
retrieved_docs.content: In 2022, the FDA approved teclistamab, the first bispecific T-cell engager antibody against CD38 expressed on the myeloma cells, for patients with relapsed or refractory multiple myeloma who have failed 4 prior lines of therapy. Belantamab mafodotin is anti-BCMA humanized immunoglobulin G (IgG) antibody conjugated with monomethyl auristatin. The drug received accelerated approval in the US for patients who had progressed on at least 4 lines of therapy. It is being withdrawn from the market based on the results of randomized phase 3 trials (DREAMM-3), as it did not show clinical benefit compared to pomalidomide and dexamethasone.

retrieved_docs.title: Ixazomib -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians must remember to monitor patients' liver function tests and platelet and neutrophil counts. The healthcare team should coordinate their activities and share information for patients on ixazomib if they are experiencing any rashes, feeling unwell, diarrhea, or other possible adverse reactions that dose modifications or other therapeutic alterations can be made in time. This interprofessional approach will increase the potential for patients to achieve success with their triple therapy, including dexamethasone and lenalidomide, and optimize outcomes in multiple myeloma cases.

retrieved_docs.title: Relapsed and Refractory Multiple Myeloma -- Treatment / Management -- Second Relapse or Subsequent Relapse
retrieved_docs.content: The penta-refractory disease is resistant or refractory to Anti-CD38 monoclonal antibodies, bortezomib, lenalidomide, pomalidomide, and carfilzomib. This is mainly encountered in the third- or fourth-line setting. If any of these agents have not been used before, it is reasonable to use that agent at any time. Patients with the penta-refractory disease have options of BMCA-targeted therapy (Ida-Cel, Cilta-Cel, or teclistamab); alkylator-based combination (VDT-PACE, DCEP, bendamustine); or combination regimens incorporating selinexor, venetoclax (only in cases with t(11;14) or panobinostat). Clinical trials or investigational agents are also encouraged.

retrieved_docs.title: Relapsed and Refractory Multiple Myeloma -- Treatment / Management -- First Relapse
retrieved_docs.content: The Castor Trial showed improved PFS with DVd compared to Vd alone (16.7 months vs 7.1 months). LEPUS trial showed similar PFS benefits in Chinese patients. Higher rates of thrombocytopenia, lymphopenia, neutropenia, and peripheral sensory neuropathy were noted. [46] OPTIMISM trial showed improved PFS (11 months vs 7 months) with VPd vs Vd. Higher rates of febrile neutropenia and thromboembolic events were noted with VPd.

retrieved_docs.title: Ixazomib -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Ixazomib is currently the only oral proteasome inhibitor being used in treating multiple myeloma, so all interprofessional healthcare team members, including physicians, specialists, advanced practice practitioners, nursing staff, and pharmacists, should be aware of this drug, its indication, dosing, adverse event profile, potential drug-drug interactions, and requisite monitoring. Due to increasing use and the many adverse effects of ixazomib, the interprofessional team must monitor patients diligently, irrespective of their specific discipline.

retrieved_docs.title: Relapsed and Refractory Multiple Myeloma -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: The current use of carfilzomib, bortezomib, and ixazomib by targeting the ubiquitin-proteosome pathway has proven an effective treatment strategy in MM patients; however, patients can develop relapse due to drug resistance. Newer agents like proteolysis targeting chimera (PROTAC) are being studied in the latest clinical trials as molecules that target the disease-related protein to induce its proteasomal degradation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1981
retrieved_docs.content: Inhibitory proteas refers to a class of proteasome‑inhibiting agents. Ixazomib is a specific drug that belongs to this inhibitory proteas class, acting as a reversible proteasome inhibitor used in the treatment of multiple myeloma. Remikiren, although primarily recognized as a renin inhibitor, is also categorized within the inhibitory proteas group because it exhibits proteasome‑inhibitory activity, placing it alongside ixazomib as another member of this therapeutic class. This relationship highlights that both ixazomib and remikiren share a common mechanism of proteasome inhibition, despite their differing primary indications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 564
retrieved_docs.content: Kyselina zoledronová and melphalan flufenamide are therapeutic agents that may be employed in the treatment of multiple myeloma. Kyselina zoledronová, a bisphosphonate, helps manage bone disease and reduce skeletal complications associated with multiple myeloma, while melphalan flufenamide, a peptide‑conjugated alkylating prodrug, provides cytotoxic activity against malignant plasma cells. The potential use of both agents underscores a multimodal approach to managing multiple myeloma, targeting both bone health and tumor burden.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 43
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple myeloma, classified as a plasma‑cell disorder (اضطراب خلايا البلازما), can be managed with a variety of therapeutic agents. The alkylating agent melfalan is reported to have activity against multiple myeloma, as are its related formulations melphalan flufenamide and melphalan hydrochloride. Immunomodulatory therapy with interferon alfa‑2a may also be employed in the treatment of this disease. The proteasome inhibitor ixazomib is another option that may treat multiple myeloma, providing a targeted approach to malignant plasma cells. Additionally, the bisphosphonate kyselina zoledronová (zoledronic acid) is used to address bone disease associated with multiple myeloma and may contribute to overall disease control. Together, these agents represent a multimodal strategy for treating multiple myeloma, reflecting its classification as a plasma‑cell disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 679
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors, direct acting antivirals constitute a class of antiviral agents that target viral proteases. Key members of this class include the protease inhibitors ritonavir, fosamprenavir, amprenavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir. Amprenavir is linked to a downstream entity termed inhibitory proteas, reflecting a broader relationship between antiviral protease inhibition and proteasome inhibition. Inhibitory proteas is classified as ixazomib, a proteasome inhibitor used in the treatment of multiple myeloma. This connection highlights the pharmacologic overlap where agents originally developed as viral protease inhibitors share mechanistic features with proteasome‑targeting drugs, informing both antiviral therapy and oncology drug development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 502
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple myeloma, classified as اضطراب خلايا البلازما (a plasma‑cell disorder), can be managed with several therapeutic agents. The alkylating agent melfalan is reported to have potential efficacy in treating multiple myeloma. Ixazomib, a proteasome inhibitor, may also be used to treat this disease. The bisphosphonate kyselina zoledronová (zoledronic acid) is another medication that may be employed in the treatment of multiple myeloma, primarily to address bone disease associated with the condition. Additionally, both melphalan flufenamide and melphalan hydrochloride, which are derivatives of the alkylating drug melphalan, are indicated as possible treatments for multiple myeloma. Together, these drugs represent a range of pharmacologic options for addressing the malignant plasma‑cell proliferation characteristic of multiple myeloma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2021
retrieved_docs.content: Gilteritinib is a targeted therapeutic agent that may be used to treat acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), offering a potential option for patients with this aggressive hematologic malignancy.

This integrated view illustrates how specific drugs are incorporated into distinct chemotherapy regimens, how they belong to larger pharmacologic classes (e.g., direct factor Xa inhibitors, Vinca‑alkaloids, alkylating agents), and how they are cataloged for the treatment of Hodgkin disease, multiple myeloma, and related conditions, supporting comprehensive patient‑care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 64
retrieved_docs.content: used in oncology (dexamethasone phosphate and the prednisolone esters listed above). Understanding these interconnections—hierarchical classifications within ATC, dual membership of agents such as anagrelide, specific mechanisms of action, therapeutic indications for leukemia, and contraindications for certain oncolytic agents—is essential for clinicians when selecting chemotherapy regimens, anticipating drug‑specific effects, and managing patient care in oncology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 308
retrieved_docs.content: Cykl​ofosfamid, an alkylating chemotherapeutic and immunosuppressive agent, may be used to treat both multiple sclerosis and multiple myeloma, illustrating its therapeutic relevance across a demyelinating autoimmune disorder and a hematologic malignancy. Its capacity to modulate immune activity and inhibit proliferating cells underlies its application in these distinct diseases, emphasizing the importance of versatile treatment options in personalized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 711
retrieved_docs.content: Karmustin, melfalan, and zoledronic acid (kyselina zoledronová) are all identified as agents that may be used to treat multiple myeloma, providing therapeutic options for managing this plasma‑cell malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 707
retrieved_docs.content: Melfalan and melphalan hydrochloride, both forms of the alkylating agent melphalan, as well as zoledronic acid (kyselina zoledronová), are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat multiple myeloma. Multiple myeloma is classified as a plasma‑cell disorder (اضطراب خلايا البلازما), representing a malignant proliferation of plasma cells. The inclusion of these drugs in treatment regimens reflects their potential efficacy in managing this specific type of plasma‑cell malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1289
retrieved_docs.content: Ibrutinib is a targeted therapeutic agent that may be used to treat سرطـان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن (chronic lymphocytic leukemia), offering a pharmacologic option for managing this hematologic malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 459
retrieved_docs.content: Melfalan, melphalan flufenamide, and melphalan hydrochloride are chemotherapeutic agents that may be used to treat multiple myeloma, a malignant plasma‑cell disorder (اضطراب خلايا البلازما). In addition, the bisphosphonate kyselina zoledronová (zoledronic acid) may also be employed in the management of multiple myeloma, particularly to address bone complications associated with the disease. Recognizing that multiple myeloma is classified under the broader category of plasma‑cell disorders highlights the relevance of these therapeutic options in targeting both the malignant plasma cells and the skeletal manifestations of the condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2212
retrieved_docs.content: Alkylating agents are classified under the category of pharmacological effects—molecular mechanisms, indicating that their therapeutic action is understood within this mechanistic framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2174
retrieved_docs.content: Hirudiny is classified as an inhibitory proteas, indicating that it functions as a proteasome‑inhibiting agent. The class of inhibitory proteas is linked to ixazomib, a well‑known proteasome inhibitor drug, and is also related to remikiren, which is identified as a member of this inhibitory proteas category. Together, hirudiny, ixazomib, and remikiren share the common mechanism of proteasome inhibition, a therapeutic strategy employed in conditions such as certain cancers where disruption of protein degradation pathways can be beneficial.

Chemotherapeutic agents melfalan, melphalan flufenamide, and melphalan hydrochloride are employed in the treatment of multiple myeloma, a malignant plasma‑cell disorder, and the bisphosphonate zoledronic acid is used to manage the associated bone disease. Together, these entities are linked to their therapeutic classes, mechanisms of action, formulation components, and relevant contraindications, providing an integrated reference for patient‑centered pharmacologic decision‑making.
